name: Fukushima Daiichi — Nuclear Safety Governance Failure
description: The 2008–2011 decision chain at TEPCO and NISA that deferred seawall upgrades despite a credible internal assessment of 15.7m tsunami risk. The plant's seawall was 5.7m. On March 11, 2011, the tsunami reached 14.1–15.5m. Source: National Diet of Japan NAIIC Report (2012), TEPCO internal documents, Funabashi & Kitazawa (2012) Bulletin of Atomic Scientists.
grounding_notes: NAIIC report (2012) is the primary authoritative source; all decision traces reconstructed from testimony. Yoshida authority override confirmed in NHK documentary and NAIIC Vol.6.

agents.name: NISA — Nuclear and Industrial Safety Agency
Tohoku Electric Power — Onagawa Plant Management

evidence.title: TEPCO Internal Tsunami Height Assessment — Jogan Earthquake Model
Geological Evidence for the 869 AD Jogan Tsunami — Deposits Found 3–4km Inland
NRC Station Blackout Rule and Extended SBO Vulnerability Studies (1988–2003)
Onagawa Plant Coastal Engineering Specification — Yanosuke Hirai Seawall Decision (14.8m)
Post-Event Tsunami Run-up Survey — Fukushima Daiichi, March 11 2011
National Diet NAIIC Final Report — The Fukushima Nuclear Accident Was a Profoundly Manmade Disaster

attributes.outcome: survived intact — became temporary evacuation shelter

attributes.alternative_proposed: elevation to higher ground, never implemented

mechanisms.name: Tsunami inundation causes station blackout
mechanisms.description: A tsunami exceeding the seawall height floods the basement where emergency diesel generators are located, causing complete station blackout and loss of all active cooling.

mechanisms.name: Regulatory capture suppresses inconvenient risk assessment
mechanisms.description: Regulatory capture at NISA and organizational risk aversion at TEPCO management created structural incentives to downplay or defer inconvenient engineering assessments, preventing timely safety upgrades.

mechanisms.name: Historical tsunami record implies future risk
mechanisms.description: Geological evidence of the 869 AD Jogan tsunami (run-up 3–4km inland) provides direct physical evidence that tsunamis of 10–15m are plausible at the Fukushima site. This evidence existed in the public geological record before 2008.

hypotheses.statement: A seawall of ≥15.7m would have prevented the critical tsunami inundation and station blackout, likely preventing core meltdown.
NISA's structural relationship with the nuclear industry (organizational capture) was causally responsible for the failure to enforce safety upgrades following the 2008 assessment.

tacit_traces.title: Yanosuke Hirai's Seawall Height Decision — Onagawa Plant, 1967
tacit_traces.description: Senior engineer Yanosuke Hirai insisted on a 14.8m seawall for the Onagawa plant against significant cost pressure, citing historical accounts of large tsunamis hitting the Sanriku coast. His reasoning combined documented historical records, local oral tradition, and engineering intuition about worst-case scenarios. This decision was not derivable from the formal regulatory requirement (which required only a much lower seawall). It reflects situated knowledge of local geological and historical context that was not formalized in any standard.

steps.action: Reviewed historical written records of Sanriku coast tsunamis
steps.rationale: Formal requirements based only on recent instrumentally-recorded events

steps.action: Consulted local fishing community oral history of large tsunami events
steps.rationale: Tacit local knowledge not in official records

steps.action: Applied 2x safety margin over largest historically documented wave
steps.rationale: Personal judgment: regulatory minimum was insufficient

steps.action: Maintained position against cost-reduction pressure over 3-year design period

tacit_traces.outcome: Onagawa plant survived the 2011 tsunami (13m run-up) with no core damage. Became a refuge for 364 disaster survivors.
tacit_traces.counterfactual: If Hirai had deferred to regulatory minimum (~3m), the plant would almost certainly have been destroyed.
tacit_traces.transferability: low — required combination of tacit local knowledge and personal conviction to override cost pressure

goals.title: Prevent radioactive release that could harm public
Maintain continuous plant operations and revenue
Minimize capital expenditure on safety upgrades
Avoid regulatory action that could trigger industry-wide upgrades

constraints.description: Plants must be protected against the design-basis tsunami, defined as the largest tsunami in the historical instrumental record. Regulatory minimum did not require consideration of paleotsunami evidence or probabilistic hazard.
TEPCO management decision: seawall upgrade deferred pending JSCE subcommittee review. No timeline specified. Effectively: until regulatory requirement forces action.

precedents.statement: Yanosuke Hirai (Tohoku Electric) insisted on a 14.8m seawall for Onagawa plant in 1967 despite regulatory minimums of ~3m, citing historical tsunami records and oral tradition. The plant survived the 2011 tsunami (13m). This precedent demonstrates that exceeding regulatory minimums based on historical evidence is both possible and consequential.

Following the 1988 NRC Station Blackout Rule, US nuclear plants were required to demonstrate capability to cope with extended station blackout for 4–8 hours. Japan did not adopt equivalent requirements. This regulatory precedent was available as a reference for NISA but was not applied.

decisions.question: Should Fukushima Daiichi's seawall be upgraded to ≥15.7m in response to the 2008 internal tsunami height assessment?
decisions.rationale: TEPCO management decided to defer the seawall upgrade, referring the matter to the JSCE Nuclear Civil Engineering Subcommittee for further study. No upgrade schedule was established. The internal assessment showing 15.7m potential tsunami height was not disclosed to the public or the Diet. The cost of the upgrade was a significant factor in the deferral decision.

decisions.question: At what height should the Onagawa nuclear plant seawall be constructed?
decisions.rationale: Senior engineer Yanosuke Hirai insisted on 14.8m seawall height based on historical tsunami records and local oral tradition, significantly exceeding the regulatory minimum. Despite cost pressure and skepticism from superiors, the decision was ultimately accepted.

dissent_records.rationale: Members of TEPCO's Civil Engineering Division who produced the 2008 tsunami assessment believed the 15.7m estimate was credible and that seawall upgrades should proceed. Their internal position was overridden by corporate management. This dissent was not given formal governance standing and was not escalated to NISA.

US NRC station blackout studies (available since 1988) demonstrated that extended loss of backup power was a credible and severe risk scenario. Japanese regulators did not adopt equivalent requirements despite this publicly available evidence. This constitutes a dissent-by-omission: the NRC's evidence and requirements were not invoked in the Japanese regulatory process.

uncertainty_annotations.dimension: credibility of 15.7m tsunami estimate at Fukushima site
uncertainty_annotations.rationale: The 2008 internal assessment used the Jogan earthquake model but the probabilistic framework was contested by JSCE subcommittee. The estimate was plausible but not consensus. Actual 2011 tsunami (14.1–15.5m) retrospectively validates the estimate.

uncertainty_annotations.dimension: degree to which regulatory capture was causally responsible vs. contributing factor
uncertainty_annotations.rationale: NAIIC report concluded the accident was fundamentally manmade due to regulatory failure, but attributing causal weight between capture, negligence, and structural issues requires careful qualification.

demo_queries.question: Why was the Fukushima Daiichi seawall not upgraded despite the 2008 tsunami assessment?
demo_queries.narrative: The 2008 internal assessment found a 15.7m tsunami was plausible. The decision to defer was made by corporate management, overriding their own civil engineering team's concerns. No timeline was set. The dissent of the engineering team was not given governance standing.

demo_queries.question: What would a governed collective intelligence system have done differently?
demo_queries.narrative: A governed system would have: surfaced the engineering team's dissent as a first-class record requiring resolution, automatically invoked the Onagawa precedent showing the viability of higher seawalls, flagged the NRC SBO rules as relevant external evidence, and blocked the deferral decision unless the uncertainty on the 15.7m estimate dropped below the safety threshold.
